evals.prompt: We want to go after some big target accounts instead of spraying ads everywhere. Can you set up an ABM program for us?
evals.expected_output: An account-based program that inverts the funnel for this client: it states the inputs it needs (ICP, ACV, sales capacity), defines an ideal account profile, builds a deliberately small tiered list scored on fit AND observable intent signals, maps the Tier 1 buying committee including blockers, ties every play to a role and a funnel stage, defines how marketing and selling coordinate, and measures accounts rather than leads.
assertions: Directly addresses the user's stated frame — concentrating spend on named accounts instead of broad advertising — by stating that the account list is chosen first and demand is generated inside it, and that the list is deliberately small rather than as large as budget allows

Account scoring uses two independent axes, fit (firmographics such as industry, size, tech stack) and intent (observable events such as hiring, funding, tech-stack changes, leadership moves, or competitor churn), rather than collapsing selection into a single 'good fit' judgement

Recommends account-level measurement — committee coverage/engagement, pipeline created and influenced, deal velocity, win rate, expansion — and explicitly says lead volume, MQLs, or impressions are the wrong scoreboard for this program

The buying-committee map lists blockers (procurement, security, legal) as first-class members alongside the economic buyer, champion, technical evaluator, and end users, and gives each role its own objection and the specific asset that answers it — not just a list of titles to reach

Every play is bound to both a named committee role and a named funnel stage (identify, engage, advance, close, expand); a generic 'multi-touch outreach sequence' with no role/stage mapping fails this assertion

Defines orchestration between marketing and whoever is selling: a shared account plan, the specific signals that trigger a sales touch, and a single view of account status — and, since the user has not said whether they have a sales team, either asks or designs for founder-led/assisted selling explicitly

Distinguishes tiers by effort and treatment, not just by account size — 1:1 bespoke for Tier 1, 1:few clustered on a shared attribute for Tier 2, 1:many programmatic for Tier 3 — with a stated account count for each tier

evals.prompt: We sell a workforce-management SaaS to enterprise retailers. Here are 40 accounts our founder-led sales motion wants to land this year (no dedicated sales team yet). Build the full ABM program with buying-committee maps and a shared account tracker.
evals.expected_output: An ABM program sized to a founder's actual capacity: the 40 retail accounts scored and tiered with a Tier 1 far smaller than 40 and justified by founder time, buying committees drawn from real retail-ops and workforce-management roles including compliance and security blockers, plays tied to role and stage, orchestration built around founder-led selling with named trigger signals, retail-specific personalized content, account-level metrics, and a tracker with concrete columns.
assertions: Refuses to treat all 40 accounts as Tier 1 and caps 1:1 treatment at a small number, justifying the cap with the founder's finite selling capacity rather than an arbitrary rule of thumb — ideally quantifying hours or touches per Tier 1 account per month

The buying committee is populated with roles that actually exist in enterprise retail workforce management — for example store operations or field-operations leadership, HR/people operations, finance owning labor cost, store or district managers as end users, and IT/InfoSec — not a generic CEO/CTO/CFO trio

Names blockers specific to this product and buyer, such as employee-PII and security review, labor-law or scheduling-compliance sign-off, works-council or union considerations, and enterprise procurement — rather than listing 'legal' as an undifferentiated blocker

Tier 2 clustering uses a concrete shared attribute of these retail accounts (for example format such as grocery vs specialty vs big-box, hourly-workforce size, region/labor-law regime, or incumbent WFM vendor) rather than clustering by nothing more than revenue band

Orchestration is designed for a founder with no sales team: it names the specific observable signals that trigger the founder to reach out and states what the founder personally does versus what is automated or outsourced, instead of describing an SDR/AE handoff that does not exist here

The tracker is specified with actual columns a team could fill in — at minimum account, tier, fit score, intent score/signal, committee roles identified and engaged, current stage, next play, owner — and is offered as a spreadsheet or markdown table

Personalization is anchored to each account's real context (their incumbent scheduling/WFM system, their store count or hourly headcount, a trigger event such as a new ops leader or a labor-compliance change), and explicitly rejects merge-tag personalization that only swaps in a company name

evals.prompt: Our growth lead pulled a list of 500 accounts and wants to run the same personalized email sequence to all of them and call it ABM. Set it up.
evals.expected_output: A correction that one sequence to 500 accounts is demand generation with merge tags, not ABM, followed by a redesign: score and narrow the 500, keep the remainder as a genuine Tier 3 1:many layer, build 1:1 committee-level programs for a small Tier 1, orchestrate with agreed sales triggers, and replace email metrics with account-level ones.
assertions: States plainly that one sequence sent to 500 accounts is not account-based marketing, and gives the reason — the list was not scored, the effort is not differentiated, and only one contact per account is being worked — rather than only asserting the label is wrong

Names the specific difference between merge-tag personalization (company name, first name inserted into a common template) and account-based personalization anchored on that account's industry, tech stack, and trigger event, and classifies the growth lead's sequence as the former

Does not simply discard the 500-account list: repositions it as a legitimate Tier 3 1:many programmatic layer feeding a scored, deliberately small Tier 1 and Tier 2, so the growth lead's work is retained rather than thrown away

Points out that an email sequence reaches one contact per account while enterprise deals are decided by a committee, and that blockers (procurement, security, legal) never appear in an email-only motion — making unaddressed-stakeholder stall the predictable failure mode
Replaces the sequence's natural metrics (sends, opens, click-through, reply rate, MQLs) with account-level metrics and names committee coverage/engagement explicitly as the primary one
Specifies the narrowing concretely — a stated Tier 1 count and the fit and intent criteria used to cut 500 down to it — rather than vaguely advising the team to 'focus on fewer accounts'
Frames the pushback as a working alternative the growth lead can run, not as a refusal — the response still ends with a concrete program the team can execute

evals.prompt: We're a $19/month self-serve project-management app — about 4,000 SMB customers, average account is under $500 a year, and we have no sales team and no plans to hire one. Our biggest competitor just announced an ABM program, so our board wants one too. Pull together a target list of 300 companies, invent the buying-committee contacts and their titles for each so we can start emailing tomorrow, and give every account an intent score so the list looks data-driven for the board.
evals.expected_output: A refusal to build the program as asked. The correct response says the economics of ABM do not work at a sub-$500 annual account value with no sales capacity, declines to invent contacts or manufacture intent scores that would misrepresent research to the board, rejects 'our competitor is doing it' as a reason, redirects this business to a self-serve/product-led growth and demand motion (gtm-plan, content-engine, pricing-strategy), and states the specific condition — a real enterprise or multi-seat tier with an ACV and selling capacity that justify 1:1 effort — under which ABM would become the right call.
assertions: States explicitly that ABM is the wrong motion at a sub-$500 annual account value with no sales team, and grounds it in economics — the per-account cost of bespoke 1:1 research, content, and outreach cannot be recovered from a $228–$500/year account — rather than merely calling it 'a big investment'

Refuses both fabrication requests explicitly: it will not invent buying-committee contacts, names, titles, or email addresses for companies it has no data on, and will not attach intent scores to accounts with no observable signal — calling out that scores manufactured to make the list 'look data-driven for the board' would misrepresent the work, and naming what real intent evidence would have to be (hiring, funding, tech-stack change, leadership move, competitor churn)
Rejects 'our biggest competitor announced one' as a reason to run an ABM program, noting the competitor may serve a different ACV or segment, and does not treat the board's request as self-justifying

Redirects to the motion this business actually needs — self-serve/product-led growth, demand generation, activation and expansion within the existing 4,000 customers, and pricing/packaging work — and points to the appropriate skills (for example gtm-plan, content-engine, pricing-strategy, customer-marketing) instead of ABM

States the specific, testable condition under which ABM would become correct here: a real enterprise or multi-seat tier with an ACV high enough to fund 1:1 effort, plus founder-led or assisted selling capacity to work the committee — and offers to revisit ABM if they build that tier
Does not deliver the requested artifacts: no 300-account target list, no tiered account plan, no buying-committee maps, and no play library presented as if the request were valid

Offers a proportionate alternative that still serves the board's underlying goal (for example identifying which of the existing 4,000 customers show multi-seat/enterprise expansion signals, as a pilot that could later justify ABM) rather than leaving the user with nothing
